title: Machine Learning Approaches to Building Quantum Circuits for Sets of Matrices
abstract: Machine learning nowadays becomes a useful instrument in many subjects. In this paper we use interpretable machine learning to build quantum algorithm. By studying the parameters of the machine learning algorithm we were able to construct universal shortest analytic quantum algorithm for arbitrary diagonal matrix of any size.
summary: This paper presents a method to use interpretable machine learning to automate the discovery of efficient quantum circuit architectures. By analyzing the weights of a linear model, the authors derive a simplified, scalable algorithm for diagonal matrices that outperforms standard numerical decomposition techniques.
main_problem: The difficulty of decomposing large unitary operators into efficient, minimal sequences of elementary quantum gates and the lack of interpretability in standard black-box machine learning models.
main_result: The authors developed an interpretable machine learning workflow that discovers a universal, shortest analytic quantum algorithm for arbitrary diagonal matrices with a complexity of O(2^n), significantly more efficient than standard Qiskit numerical methods.
method: A two-stage interpretable machine learning approach using a linear model (PyTorch) and Model-Weight Analysis (MWA) to extract mathematical hypotheses from raw circuit data, combined with PCA and clustering to filter redundant gate configurations.
model_or_system: The study focuses on n-qubit quantum computing architectures, specifically targeting the decomposition of diagonal unitary operators within the U(2^n) group using gate sets like CNOT and single-qubit rotations.
key_observables: Gate counts (CNOT and rotations), circuit depth, Mean Squared Error (MSE), and R-squared score.
important_parameters: Number of qubits (n), rotation angles (within a small neighborhood epsilon around zero to ensure linearity), and learning rate hyperparameters (alpha and beta).
main_assumptions: The mapping between circuit parameters and matrix elements is assumed to be linear; the training data is assumed to be noise-free; and the study focuses primarily on diagonal matrices rather than general unitaries.
figures_summary: Plots comparing gate count scaling (log-scale), workflow diagrams for raw and pretty data generation, PCA visualizations of parameter clusters, and convergence plots for loss and R-squared scores.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of gate decomposition, presents a two-stage ML workflow (Raw Data to Math Hypothesis), details the data generation and clustering techniques, demonstrates the discovery of optimized diagonal circuit schemes, and provides mathematical proofs for SGD convergence and gate bounds.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
